talk_title: Adolescent adherence to Planetary Health Dietary Index and breast cancer risk

speaker.name: Phuong Anh Le
speaker.affiliation: Harvard Chan School of Public Health

biomarkers.name: ER-negative status
biomarkers.finding: Adolescent high PHDI associated with >30% reduced risk of ER-negative breast cancer (HR ~0.70)

models.name: Nurses' Health Study II
models.genotype_or_key_features: 47,000 women, recalled adolescent diet, prospective follow-up 1998-2019

clinical_data.metric: hazard ratio for ER-negative breast cancer

MOA_summary: Higher adherence to the EAT-Lancet planetary health diet during adolescence is linked to a lower incidence of ER-negative breast cancer, particularly among premenopausal women with lower BMI, suggesting a protective effect of plant-forward dietary patterns on hormone‑negative tumor development.
key_findings: Adolescent high PHDI reduced overall invasive breast cancer risk by >30% for ER-negative tumors.
Association remained significant after adjustment for weight change since age 18.
Effect was strongest in premenopausal women and those with BMI <21 kg/m2 at age 18.
Whole fruits were protective; added saturated and trans fats increased risk.
open_questions: Would consistent adherence to the diet from adolescence through adulthood further reduce risk?
Can intermediate biomarkers (e.g., mammographic density) clarify the causal pathway?

talk_title: Cardiorespiratory multimorbidity and lung cancer risk in the UK Biobank

speaker.name: Wang Peng
speaker.affiliation: Tianjin Medical University Cancer Institute and Hospital

models.name: UK Biobank
models.genotype_or_key_features: Middle-aged adults, median follow-up 13.7 years, free of cancer/CRD/CVD at baseline

clinical_data.metric: hazard ratio for lung cancer (cardiorespiratory multimorbidity)
clinical_data.population: participants with both CRD and CVD

clinical_data.metric: hazard ratio for transition from CRM to lung cancer
risk reduction with healthy lifestyle (CRM to lung cancer)

MOA_summary: Co-occurrence of chronic respiratory disease and cardiovascular disease markedly increases lung cancer incidence, with a hazard ratio of 4.11 versus healthy individuals, and healthy lifestyle factors (non‑smoking, diet) attenuate progression risk by up to 57%.
key_findings: Cardiorespiratory multimorbidity (CRM) confers the highest lung cancer risk (HR 4.11) compared with single disease states.
22% of CRD patients progressed to CRM versus 4.9% of CVD patients.
CRM shows the shortest transition time to lung cancer among disease states.
Healthy lifestyle reduces progression from single disease to CRM by 34‑54% and from CRM to lung cancer by 57%.
open_questions: How to account for reverse causality between health status and lifestyle adoption?
What are optimal thresholds for lifestyle scores to maximize risk reduction?

talk_title: Generational immigration status and gastric cancer incidence among Japanese descendants

models.name: Multi-Ethnic Cohort Study
models.genotype_or_key_features: 94,000 participants of Japanese ancestry and NHW, median follow-up ~20 years, 716 gastric cancer cases

clinical_data.metric: hazard ratio for gastric cancer (first generation vs NHW)
hazard ratio for gastric cancer (third generation vs NHW)

MOA_summary: Risk of gastric cancer remains elevated across generations of Japanese immigrants in the US, decreasing from a five‑fold increase in first‑generation to a 2.5‑fold increase in third‑generation, indicating persistent ancestry‑related susceptibility beyond environmental assimilation.
key_findings: First‑generation Japanese immigrants have ~5× higher gastric cancer risk than NHW.
Risk declines across generations but remains ~2.5× higher in third generation.
Pattern is strongest for non‑cardia gastric cancer.
No effect modification by family history, education, BMI, smoking, or sodium intake.
open_questions: Would targeted gastric cancer screening be cost‑effective for high‑risk immigrant groups?
How does H. pylori prevalence across generations influence residual risk?

talk_title: PRIME: Parsimonious EHR risk model for pancreatic cancer

speaker.name: Lucas Mavromatis
speaker.affiliation: NYU Grossman School of Medicine, Division of Precision Medicine

models.name: OptumLabs Data Warehouse
models.genotype_or_key_features: 4.9M training, 31 health systems validation, adults ≥40 y, BMI measured 2016‑2018

clinical_data.metric: absolute 3‑year PDAC risk for top 1% PRIME

clinical_data.value: 5.8% of all PDAC cases within 3 years

MOA_summary: An interpretable elastic‑net derived EHR model (PRIME) using 19 routine clinical variables predicts pancreatic cancer risk with AUC ≈0.75, identifying a high‑risk 1% subgroup with a 0.61% three‑year absolute risk, comparable to germline mutation carriers.
key_findings: PRIME achieved AUC 0.76 (24 mo) and 0.75 (36 mo) in US validation health systems.
Top 1% risk group has 0.61% 3‑year absolute PDAC risk (1 in 128) and captures 5.8% of incident cases.
Model performance modestly declines in UK Biobank (AUC 0.71) but remains well‑calibrated.
High‑risk individuals have similar prevalence of known PDAC pathogenic germline variants as the general population, indicating capture of sporadic risk.

combination_strategies.rationale: Sequential case‑finding: define high‑risk then apply blood biomarker
Alternative blood biomarker with higher specificity
combination_strategies.phenotype: Higher PPV but fewer cancers identified

open_questions: What absolute risk threshold optimally balances detection yield and resource use?
How will prospective implementation affect patient uptake and downstream imaging burden?

talk_title: Basal metabolic rate and survival in colorectal cancer (COLO-CARE)

speaker.name: Sheetal Hardikar
speaker.affiliation: University of Utah, Huntsman Cancer Institute

models.name: COLO-CARE study
models.genotype_or_key_features: 3,760 CRC patients, US and Germany, all stages, BMR calculated from weight/height/age/sex

clinical_data.metric: hazard ratio for overall survival (highest BMR quartile vs lowest)
hazard ratio for CRC‑specific survival (highest BMR quartile vs lowest)
overall survival benefit in younger men (<50 y)

MOA_summary: Higher predicted basal metabolic rate, reflecting greater physiological reserve, is associated with improved overall and colorectal‑cancer‑specific survival, especially pronounced in younger male patients.
key_findings: Patients in the highest BMR quartile had 23% lower risk of overall death (HR 0.77).
Highest BMR quartile showed 36% lower risk of CRC‑specific death (HR 0.64).
Benefit was strongest in men under 50, with ~70% better survival.
BMR correlated positively with BMI and was higher in men than women.
open_questions: Can serial BMR measurements track cachexia development and guide interventions?
How does BMR integrate with imaging‑derived body composition metrics?

talk_title: Telehealth utilization disparities among older cancer patients

models.name: Medicare fee‑for‑service claims (2019‑2021)
models.genotype_or_key_features: 1.1 M cancer patients, 6 cancer types, ~70 M oncology claims

clinical_data.metric: overall telehealth utilization within first year of diagnosis
telehealth use by race (Black vs NHW)

MOA_summary: Older cancer patients exhibit marked disparities in telehealth adoption, with lower uptake among Black, low‑income, and non‑English‑speaking groups, and provider/system factors (e.g., academic setting, female providers) influencing delivery.
readouts: E&M CPT codes with telehealth modifiers
key_findings: 14.1% of older cancer patients used telehealth at least once in the first year post‑diagnosis.
Utilization peaked in 2020 (21.2%) then declined to 16.2% in 2021.
Black patients had the lowest utilization (11.2%); Asian/Pacific Islanders highest (17.9%).
Provider type (APPs, female clinicians) and academic facilities increased telehealth delivery.
open_questions: What specific barriers (digital literacy, broadband access) drive lower use in vulnerable groups?
How can provider‑level interventions improve sustained telehealth engagement?

talk_title: CONFLUENCE: Multi‑ancestry GWAS of breast cancer

models.name: CONFLUENCE consortium
models.genotype_or_key_features: ≈227,000 breast cancer cases, 1.7 M controls, 5 ancestries, 45 M variants

clinical_data.metric: number of independent risk loci identified
AUC improvement of CONFLUENCE PRS vs 313‑variant PRS (UK Biobank EUR)

MOA_summary: A massive multi‑ancestry GWAS (CONFLUENCE) uncovered 545 independent breast cancer risk loci, including 273 novel signals, and a 544‑variant PRS derived from these loci outperforms the standard 313‑variant score across European, African, and admixed American populations.
key_findings: CONFLUENCE includes >2 M participants, tripling effective sample size of prior multi‑ancestry studies.
Identified 545 independent loci, of which 273 are novel.
A 544‑variant PRS improves AUC by 2‑3% across EUR, AFR, and AMR cohorts versus the 313‑variant PRS.
Resource will be publicly released with summary statistics and data access platform.
open_questions: How to calibrate PRS thresholds across ancestries for clinical risk stratification?
Can integration with lifestyle and clinical factors enhance predictive performance?
